elements.sent-2411-002: Generating high-quality stereo videos that mimic human binocular vision requires maintaining consistent depth perception and temporal coherence across frames.
elements.sent-2411-003: While diffusion models have advanced image and video synthesis, generating high-quality stereo videos remains challenging due to the difficulty of maintaining consistent temporal and spatial coherence between left and right views.
elements.sent-2411-004: We introduce StereoCrafter-Zero, a novel framework for zero-shot stereo video generation that leverages video diffusion priors without the need for paired training data.
elements.sent-2411-005: Key innovations include a noisy restart strategy to initialize stereo-aware latents and an iterative refinement process that progressively harmonizes the latent space, addressing issues like temporal flickering and view inconsistencies.
elements.sent-2411-006: Comprehensive evaluations, including quantitative metrics and user studies, demonstrate that StereoCrafter-Zero produces high-quality stereo videos with improved depth consistency and temporal smoothness, even when depth estimations are imperfect.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our framework is robust and adaptable across various diffusion models, setting a new benchmark for zero-shot stereo video generation and enabling more immersive visual experiences.
elements.sent-2411-009: There is a renewed interest in stereo images and videos due to the recent release of new hardware for head-mounted displays.
elements.sent-2411-010: Stereo image conversion aims to reconstruct stereo pairs from single images.
elements.sent-2411-011: Inconsistent depth information can lead to visual artifacts and a lack of coherence between frames, undermining the immersive experience that stereo videos are intended to provide.
elements.sent-2411-012: Previous works~\cite{xie2016deep3d, wang2019web, Shih3DP20, watson2020learning, Ranftl2022 have significantly advanced the ability to generate left and right views that maintain depth consistency and realistic parallax effects.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Building upon these foundations, recent studies have extended stereo conversion techniques to video, emphasizing the importance of temporal consistency to produce smooth and coherent stereo video sequences \cite{shi2024immersepro, zhao2024stereocrafter .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Concurrently, diffusion models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for generative tasks, demonstrating remarkable proficiency in capturing complex data distributions and generating high-fidelity images and videos~\cite{ho2020denoising, rombach2022high, ramesh2022hierarchical .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Notably, StereoDiffusion~\cite{wang2024stereodiffusion presented a zero-shot approach that leveraged diffusion models to generate stereo images, showcasing the potential of creating stereo content with generative models without training.}
elements.sent-2411-016: In this work, we explore a relatively unexplored field of stereo video generation with diffusion models in a zero-shot manner without significant computational requirements.
elements.sent-2411-017: Stereo video generation presents unique challenges that differentiate it from single-image stereo.
elements.sent-2411-018: Temporal flickering and view inconsistencies between the left and right views are common issues that degrade the user experience.
elements.sent-2411-019: Therefore, ensuring temporal coherence across frames when extending these methods to video remains a significant challenge, which requires meticulous handling of depth cues to ensure realistic parallax effects and maintaining temporal-consistent depth perception across both left and right views~\cite{barron2015structure .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Notably, recent progress in video depth estimation models improves the temporal consistency by accurately capturing and maintaining depth information across video frames~\cite{luo2020consistent,kopf2021robust,hu2024-DepthCrafter .}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, the challenge lies in the nuanced interplay between depth consistency and temporal coherence within the context of diffusion-based stereo video generation.
elements.sent-2411-022: To be specific, maintaining consistency not only between consecutive frames (temporal) but also between the left and right views within each frame (inter-view) is essential.
elements.sent-2411-023: Current video generation models primarily focus on overall visual fidelity and temporal smoothness without explicitly enforcing depth consistency between the left and right views across consecutive frames.
elements.sent-2411-024: Existing methods lack mechanisms to dynamically adapt depth information within the diffusion process to account for scene dynamics for stereo video generation.
elements.sent-2411-025: Addressing these challenges, we introduce StereoCrafter-Zero, a novel framework for zero-shot stereo video generation that leverages video diffusion priors to generate high-quality stereo videos without the need for paired training data.
elements.sent-2411-026: Instead of performing the stereo conversion on the video level, we improve and refine the consistency within the latent features of a video diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-027: Our approach seamlessly integrates advanced video depth estimation into the diffusion-based synthesis process, ensuring depth consistency and temporal coherence.
elements.sent-2411-028: We carry out quantitative and qualitative studies that demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, including a user study.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our main contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-030: To enhance the latent consistency, we utilize a noisy restart strategy to obtain stereo-aware initial latents.
elements.sent-2411-031: Subsequently, an iterative refinement process systematically injects controlled noise into the diffusion process, progressively improving the harmony of the latent space.
elements.sent-2411-032: We present comprehensive statistical evaluation and user studies, demonstrating our method can generate high-quality stereo videos.
elements.sent-2411-033: Diffusion models have achieved great success in text-to-image generation ~\citep{rombach2022high,esser2024scaling,balaji2022eDiff-I .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Recently, diffusion models have been widely adopted in the video domain to produce high-quality videos given text prompts or images as input ~\citep{ho2022imagenvideohighdefinition,xing2023dynamicrafter,blattmann2023svd,yang2024cogvideox,ho2022video_diffusion_models,sora .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Stable Video Diffusion~\citep{blattmann2023svd collected and curated a large-scale video dataset to enable video generation with competitive performance.}
elements.sent-2411-036: DynamiCrafter~\citep{xing2023dynamicrafter proposed to use dual-stream image injection to incorporate images as guidance and can animate input images complemented with text prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-037: These models form the foundation of our proposed method for stereo video generation.
elements.sent-2411-038: However, despite these advances, generating stereo video with synchronized left and right views remains a challenge.
elements.sent-2411-039: Existing methods fail to account for the stereo disparity between views and often suffer from temporal flickering, especially in dynamic scenes.
elements.sent-2411-040: Novel-view synthesis aims to generate images rendered under a novel target camera pose from given source images.
elements.sent-2411-041: Recent novel-view synthesis works such as ~\cite{Li_2023_CVPR,liu2023robust,yu2023hifi,tang2023make,sun2023dreamcraft3d,yu2024viewcrafter may achieve a similar goal to create a stereo pair of a given scene, but they require scene-specific optimization which is not suitable to apply on video data.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Another branch of works~\cite{shriram2024realmdreamer,chung2023luciddreamer,zhang2024text2nerf uses depth-warping to synthesize novel views and refines the warped images.}
elements.sent-2411-043: These approaches may suffer from artifacts in the inpainted regions.
elements.sent-2411-044: More importantly, handling video-based novel view synthesis may additionally require temporal consistency.
elements.sent-2411-045: CVD~\citep{kuang2024cvd utilized a cross-video synchronization module to directly generate multi-view videos given a target camera trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Another line of work~\citep{yu20244real,bahmani20244dfy,zhang20244diffusion explicitly constructed 3D scenes along with temporal changes.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Although they can generate novel videos, the domain is limited to simple object-centric scenes and lower visual quality.
elements.sent-2411-048: It still remains a challenge to generate novel-view videos with complex visual content.
elements.sent-2411-049: Stereo content generation has evolved significantly from traditional disparity estimation and view synthesis methods to advanced deep learning techniques that enhance depth accuracy and visual realism.
elements.sent-2411-050: Early approaches utilized neural networks and generative networks to create stereo image pairs by predicting disparity maps and synthesizing corresponding views~\cite{xie2016deep3d, wang2019web .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Recent advancements have extended these techniques to video, addressing temporal coherence to ensure smooth and immersive stereo video sequences ~\cite{zhang2022temporal3d,shi2024immersepro, zhao2024stereocrafter .}
elements.sent-2411-052: Concurrently, diffusion models have been successfully adapted for stereo image generation, enabling zero-shot synthesis~\cite{wang2024stereodiffusion .}
elements.sent-2411-053: However, zero-shot generative models for stereo video are still under exploration, particularly in ensuring both spatial depth fidelity and temporal consistency across frames.
elements.sent-2411-054: A concurrent work~\cite{dai2024svg proposes a frame matrix by placing multiple camera views and warps the latent on every DDIM sampling step.}
elements.sent-2411-055: In contrast, our method can generate new viewpoints without the need to perform warping at every step, which can highly reduce the computational cost.

elements.sent-2411-056: Image Warping for Stereo View Synthesis Generating a stereo pair of a single input image includes warping the image based on a disparity map.
elements.sent-2411-057: The disparity map $d$ can be computed using the depth map of the image: \begin{equation}d(x,y)=\frac{f\cdot B}{I_{depth}(x,y)}, \label{eq:disparity}\end{equation} where $f$ is the focal length, $B$ is an adjustable baseline distance, and $I_{depth (x,y)$ is the depth value for a pixel at the location $(x,y)$.}
elements.sent-2411-058: With the disparity map, the right-view image can be obtained by warping the original 2D image with: \begin{equation}I_{right}(x-d(x,y),y)=I(x,y). \label{eq:warp}\end{equation} This shift creates a horizontal parallax, mimicking the viewpoint difference.
elements.sent-2411-059: However, the warping operation requires a high precision of the depth map and can introduce artifacts and misalignments.
elements.sent-2411-060: Due to occlusions, some pixels in the right view may not have a corresponding pixel in the original image.
elements.sent-2411-061: Previous works~\cite{watson-2020-stereo-from-mono,mehl2024stereo,zhao2024stereocrafter applied inpainting to handle the occluded and disoccluded areas.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Stereo Pair Generation with Diffusion Models.
elements.sent-2411-063: Recent works such as~\cite{wang2024stereodiffusion propose to warp the latent space of an image diffusion model.}
elements.sent-2411-064: This can be achieved since the early-stage sampling in diffusion models involves low-frequency signals that primarily define the contours of the generated image.
elements.sent-2411-065: When the step $t$ is large, high-frequency signals in the later stage sampling refine the image details.
elements.sent-2411-066: Formally, the generative process for each DDIM step can be described as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} x_{t-1} = \sqrt{\alpha_{t-1}}\big( \frac{x_t-\sqrt{1-\alpha_t}\epsilon_\theta^t(x_t)}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}} + \sqrt{1-\alpha_{t-1}-\sigma_t^2\epsilon_\theta^{(t)}(x_t)} + \sigma_t \epsilon_t, \end{aligned} \label{eq:ddim}\end{equation} where $\epsilon_t$ is noise following a standard Gaussian distribution $\mathcal{N (0, {I )$, independent of $x_t$, and $\alpha_t$ controls the noise scale at step $t$ with $\alpha_0:=1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: As the only randomness during the diffusion process, we duplicate the $\epsilon$ for different views by default in our work.
elements.sent-2411-068: Meanwhile, as demonstrated in~\cref{sec:noisy_restart,sec:exp , we found the noise level $\epsilon$ plays an important role in creating stereoscopic effects.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Cross-View Attention To enhance multi-view consistency, many works~\cite{cao2023masactrl,chen2023fec,chen2024anydoor,huang2023kv,khandelwal2023infusion,mou2023dragondiffusion,nam2024dreammatcher,wang2024stereodiffusion modify the diffusion model's attention mechanism by replacing the query, key, and value pairs, ensuring coherent generation across different views.}
elements.sent-2411-070: In this work, we allow the left view's query (Q) to attend directly to the right view's key (K) and value (V).
elements.sent-2411-071: This mechanism promotes stronger alignment and coherence across views by sharing essential spatial and contextual information.
elements.sent-2411-072: Let $Q_L, K_L, V_L$ and $Q_R, K_R, V_R$ represent the query, key, and value matrices for the left view and right view, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-073: To align the right view with the left view’s features, we use $Q_L $ (from the left view) to attend to $K_R$ and $V_R$ (from the right view).
elements.sent-2411-074: Mathematically, the right view feature $F_{R \to L}$ can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\begin{split} F_{R \to L} = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{Q_L K_R^\top}{\sqrt{d}}\right) \cdot V_R. \end{split}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-075: In this section, we describe the StereoCrafter-Zero framework for zero-shot stereo video generation, which leverages video diffusion priors to generate stereo videos.

elements.sent-2411-089: The noisy restart mechanism is implemented over $K$ diffusion steps for $N$ iterations.
elements.sent-2411-090: We denote the $K$ diffusion step as $\{ x_t \}_{t=0 ^{K}$, where $x_t $represents the latent state at step $t$.}
elements.sent-2411-091: Note that $K$ can be a set of discrete timesteps, while we use $K=\{0,...,5\}$.
elements.sent-2411-092: The general idea of the noisy start is to introduce random noise to the filling latent to prevent structural repetition and promote smooth transitions.
elements.sent-2411-093: So that the first iteration generates $K$ filling latents for each diffusion step, while the future iterations will keep iterating on the obtained $K$ latents.
elements.sent-2411-094: Refer to~\cref{eq:ddim , a random noise $\epsilon_t$ is introduced in each sampling step.}
elements.sent-2411-095: This randomness of $\epsilon_t$ may break the consistency between multiple iterations if each iteration uses different random noises.
elements.sent-2411-096: In order to avoid it in the noisy restart stage, prior to the start of each diffusion sampling sequence, we initialize fixed noise tensors $\epsilon{' _t$, then set the same random seed for each iteration.}
elements.sent-2411-097: This operation keeps the same random generator status across multiple iterations, which ensures the noise injection is purely controled by $\epsilon'_t$ and $\alpha_t$ in~\cref{eq:noisy_restart .}
elements.sent-2411-098: Meanwhile, such a controlled approach provides structural stability throughout the generation process, ensuring consistent low-frequency noise that preserves major features across frames.
elements.sent-2411-099: We showcase the effectiveness of this method in~\cref{sec:exp .}
elements.sent-2411-100: In our work, we set a total of $L = 7$ iterations, and the first iteration directly uses the left-view latents as the filling latents.
elements.sent-2411-101: The noise is injected into the latent state $x_{t+1 $with a weighted addition, balancing the influence of the existing latent and the injected noise.}
elements.sent-2411-102: The update equation for $x_{t-1}$ becomes: \begin{equation}x_{t-1} = x_{t} \cdot (1 - \alpha_t) + \sigma_t \epsilon{'}_t \alpha_t \label{eq:noisy_restart}\end{equation} where $\sigma_t $is the noise magnitude for each timestep, scaling the impact of injected noise, and we use $\alpha_t$ as the balancing coefficient, controlling the mix between the latent state and the noise.

elements.sent-2411-103: Abrupt Border Handling Direct warping of the right view can result in blank regions along the right border.
elements.sent-2411-104: These blank areas may create irrelevant or distracting content in the border regions during the iterative process.
elements.sent-2411-105: A border mask, $ {M _{\text{border $, is created by a heuristic function to select the border areas.}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: We then inpaint the border with the corresponding regions in the left view latents, $ {L _l $.}
elements.sent-2411-107: The resulting border refined latent $ {L _{r,\text{refined $ is defined as: \begin{equation}{L}_{r,\text{refined}} = {L}_r \cdot (1 - {M}_{\text{border}}) + {L}_l \cdot {M}_{\text{border}}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: This operation replaces the blank columns in the right view with the content from the left view, ensuring that the border remains visually continuous and free from distracting artifacts.

elements.sent-2411-114: The iterative refinement is implemented by repeating the denoising operation at specific sampling steps.
elements.sent-2411-115: At these sampling steps, the model performs multiple iterations to progressively enhance the latent representation.
elements.sent-2411-116: This mechanism enables the model to focus on refining details and correcting errors introduced by the warping operation and earlier steps.
elements.sent-2411-117: The iterative refinement process is encapsulated in a loop that performs key operations for each propagation step \( j \) within the total number of refinement iterations.
elements.sent-2411-118: Each iteration produces the predicted clean latent \( \hat{x _0^j \), scaling factors \( \sqrt{\bar{\alpha ^j \), directional term \( {d ^j \), noise \( \boldsymbol{\epsilon ^j \), and the latent $x_t^j$ for the next iteration.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: Starting from the second iteration $j > 1$, we update the filling area with the following equation: { \begin{equation} \hat{{x}}_0'^j = \begin{cases} \hat{{x}}_0'^{j} * \sqrt{\alpha_t} + \hat{{x}}_0'^{j-1} * (1 - \sqrt{\alpha_t}) \text{if } x_t^{blank}=1, \hat{{x}}_0'^{j-1} \text{otherwise}. \end{cases} \label{eq:blank_areas_itrefine}\end{equation} Then the latent is reconstructed by combining the normalized prediction with the directional term and noise: \begin{equation}\hat{x}_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}} \, \hat{{x}}_0'^j + {d}_{xt} + {\epsilon}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-120: Since the latents need to go through the same diffusion denoising process multiple times, we first make sure the predicted latent \( \hat{x _t \) can maintain statistical consistency across iterations.}

elements.sent-2411-123: We then simply rescale the latent with $\hat{x _t = x_t \times s$.}
elements.sent-2411-124: This scaling adjusts the noise level appropriately, maintaining the stability and convergence of the diffusion process.

elements.sent-2411-176: We present a vectorization technique for efficiently implementing pixel-wise image transformations that involve pixel displacement maps.
elements.sent-2411-177: Traditional implementations of such transformations rely on nested loops to process each pixel individually, which can be computationally intensive and inefficient, especially for high-resolution images or large batches.
elements.sent-2411-178: Our method addresses these inefficiencies by leveraging advanced tensor operations in PyTorch to vectorize the computations while ensuring the correctness of the output, particularly in handling overlapping pixel assignments and maintaining the original assignment order.
elements.sent-2411-179: Problem Formulation Let $I \in \mathbb{R ^{B \times C \times H \times W}$ be a batch of input images, where $B$ is the batch size, $C$ is the number of channels, and $H$ and $W$ are the height and width of the images, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-180: Let $D \in \mathbb{R ^{B \times 1 \times H \times W}$ be the corresponding batch of depth maps or optical flow fields.}
elements.sent-2411-181: The goal is to create a derived image $I'$ by displacing pixels from the input image $I$ based on the values in $D$, scaled by a factor $s$ and an exponent $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-182: This transformation can be formalized as: \begin{equation}I'_{b, c, h, w'} = I_{b, c, h, w}, \quad \text{where} \quad w' = w + \text{offset}_{b, h, w},\end{equation} \begin{equation}\text{offset}_{b, h, w} = \left( D_{b, 0, h, w}^\alpha \times s \right).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}I'_{b, c, h, w'} = I_{b, c, h, w}, \quad where \quad w' = w + offset_{b, h, w},\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}offset_{b, h, w} = \left( D_{b, 0, h, w}^\alpha \times s \right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-183: The challenge arises when multiple source pixels $(b, c, h, w)$ map to the same destination pixel $(b, c, h, w')$, leading to overlapping assignments.
elements.sent-2411-184: It is crucial to ensure that the final value of $I'$ at any pixel $(b, c, h, w')$ corresponds to the last assignment in the original processing order, consistent with the behavior of the nested loops.
elements.sent-2411-185: Baseline Implementation The traditional implementation uses three nested loops over the batch, height, and width dimensions: { .}
elements.verbatim-2411-001: for batch in range(B): for row in range(H): for col in range(W): col_d = col + D[ batch, 0, row, col] ** alpha * s if 0 <= col_d < W: I_prime[batch, :, row, col_d] = I[batch, :, row, col]
elements.sent-2411-186: This approach ensures correct handling of overlapping assignments by processing pixels in a specific order (e.g., from right to left when $s \geq 0$) and overwriting previous values as needed.
elements.sent-2411-187: However, it is computationally inefficient due to the explicit loops.
elements.sent-2411-188: Proposed Vectorization Technique Our method eliminates the explicit loops by vectorizing operations across the batch, height, and width dimensions using PyTorch tensors.
elements.sent-2411-189: The key challenge is to replicate the assignment order of the nested loops to correctly handle overlapping assignments.
elements.sent-2411-190: First, we create tensors representing the batch, row, and column indices for all pixels.
elements.sent-2411-191: For positive movement such as $s \geq 0$, we process columns from right to left ($W - 1$ to $0$), while we process columns from left to right ($0$ to $W - 1$) for $s < 0$.
elements.sent-2411-192: For each column indices $col$, we then compute the offset $D$ for each pixel and calculate the destination column indices $col_d=col + D$.
elements.sent-2411-193: Next, a Boolean mask is applied to filter out indices where $\text{col\_d}$ is outside the image boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-194: Note that the order for all valid indices is reversed to prioritize the last assignments in the original loop order.
elements.sent-2411-195: This conditional reversal ensures that, when duplicates are removed, the last assignment in the original loop order is preserved, correctly handling overlapping assignments.
elements.sent-2411-196: Next, we compute unique destination indices and keep the first occurrence, which corresponds to the last assignment due to reversal.
elements.sent-2411-197: We can then simply apply the computed index map to assign all pixels in bulk.
elements.sent-2411-198: Experimental Validation We validate our method by comparing the outputs and execution times of the original and vectorized implementations, as shown in \Cref{tab:exp_vec .}
elements.sent-2411-199: The values are computed with $20$ executions for each run.
elements.sent-2411-200: The results show that our implementation offers an effective solution for efficiently implementing pixel-wise image transformations that involve displacement based on depth maps or optical flow fields.

elements.section-2411-010: Perfect Depth Maps Can Be Imperfect
elements.sent-2411-224: This section introduces a disparity propagation approach that can generate depth maps with consistent depth values, then we compare this solution against the video depth estimation approach.
elements.sent-2411-225: With an aim of a more stable video with fewer artifacts, we first obtain a video depth map that allows smoother transitions and minimizes abrupt depth changes.
elements.sent-2411-226: Therefore, we propose disparity propagation, that propagates depth information from a single, initial depth frame across the entire video using frame-by-frame optical flow warping.
elements.sent-2411-227: This approach provides a smoother, more consistent disparity map over time, as it mitigates frame-specific artifacts by avoiding the per-frame recalculation of depth.
elements.sent-2411-228: Given an initial depth map $D_t$ for the frame at time $t$, we estimate the depth map at the previous $t+1$ frame and the next $t-1$ frame by warping $D_t$ based on the forward and backward optical flow, $F_{t \to t+1}$ and $F_{t \to t-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-229: We use a warping operation to apply the optical flow $F_{t \to t+1}$ to adjust $D_t$ based on the motion between the frames.
elements.sent-2411-230: The forward warping function can be expressed as: \begin{equation}D_{t+1}(x, y) = D_t\big(x + F_{t \to t+1}^x(x, y), \; y + F_{t \to t+1}^y(x, y)\big).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}D_{t+1}(x, y) = D_t\big(x + F_{t \to t+1}^x(x, y), \; y + F_{t \to t+1}^y(x, y)\big).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-231: Note that the backward warping $D_t$ to $D_{t-1}$ can also be achieved with this operation since $F_{t+1 \to t = - F_{t \to t+1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-232: We repeat the process for each precedent and subsequent frame to propagate the initial depth across the entire video sequence.
elements.sent-2411-233: This produces a sequence of disparity maps $\{D_0, D_1, \dots, D_T\}$ that are inherently smooth and consistent over time.
elements.subsection-2411-011: Disparity Propagation vs. Video Depth Estimation
elements.sent-2411-234: Existing video depth estimation models often overemphasize subtle scene movements, such as the drift of particles or minor background elements.
elements.sent-2411-235: Commonly, such objects are fine-grained high-frequency information that is not exhibited in the early stages of a diffusion latent representation.
elements.sent-2411-236: As shown in~\Cref{fig:latent_space , the early latent spaces mostly emphasize the low-frequency structure information.}

elements.sent-2411-237: The direct use of video depth estimation results may mistakenly warp the low-frequency latent component using the prediction of high-frequency depth maps.

elements.sent-2411-255: Generating high-quality stereo videos that mimic human binocular vision requires consistent depth perception and temporal coherence across frames.
elements.sent-2411-256: Despite advances in image and video synthesis using diffusion models, producing high-quality stereo videos remains a challenging task due to the difficulty of maintaining consistent temporal and spatial coherence between left and right views.
elements.sent-2411-257: We introduce StereoCrafter-Zero, a novel framework for zero-shot stereo video generation that leverages video diffusion priors without requiring paired training data.
elements.sent-2411-258: Our key innovations include a noisy restart strategy to initialize stereo-aware latent representations and an iterative refinement process that progressively harmonizes the latent space, addressing issues like temporal flickering and view inconsistencies.
elements.sent-2411-259: In addition, we propose the use of dissolved depth maps to streamline latent space operations by reducing high-frequency depth information.
elements.sent-2411-260: Our comprehensive evaluations, including quantitative metrics and user studies, demonstrate that StereoCrafter-Zero produces high-quality stereo videos with enhanced depth consistency and temporal smoothness, even when depth estimations are imperfect.
elements.sent-2411-262: The resurgence of head-mounted displays has renewed interest in stereo images and videos.
elements.sent-2411-263: Stereo image conversion, which aims to reconstruct stereo pairs from single images, has progressed significantly.
elements.sent-2411-264: However, inconsistent depth information can cause visual artifacts and incoherence between frames, undermining the immersive experience that stereo videos are intended to provide.
elements.sent-2411-265: Previous works~\cite{xie2016deep3d, wang2019web, Shih3DP20, watson2020learning, Ranftl2022 have improved depth consistency and parallax effects in stereo image generation.}
elements.sent-2411-266: Recent studies have extended stereo conversion techniques to video, emphasizing the importance of temporal consistency to produce smooth and coherent stereo video sequences \cite{shi2024immersepro, zhao2024stereocrafter .}
elements.sent-2411-267: Meanwhile, diffusion models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for generative tasks, demonstrating remarkable proficiency in capturing complex data distributions and creating high-fidelity images and videos~\cite{ho2020denoising, rombach2022high, ramesh2022hierarchical .}
elements.sent-2411-268: Notably, StereoDiffusion~\cite{wang2024stereodiffusion demonstrated the potential of zero-shot stereo image generation using diffusion models.}
elements.sent-2411-269: In this work, we explore the relatively unexplored field of stereo video generation with diffusion models in a zero-shot manner without significant computational requirements.
elements.sent-2411-270: Generating stereo video is more complex than generating stereo images, requiring meticulous handling of depth cues to produce realistic parallax and consistent depth perception in both views~\cite{barron2015structure .}
elements.sent-2411-271: Recently, video depth estimation models have improved temporal consistency by accurately capturing and maintaining depth information across video frames~\cite{luo2020consistent,kopf2021robust,hu2024-DepthCrafter,chen2025video .}
elements.sent-2411-272: However, for stereo video generation, the core issue lies in the nuanced interplay between depth consistency and temporal coherence, requiring models to maintain consistency both across consecutive frames (temporal consistency) and between the left and right views within each frame (inter-view consistency).
elements.sent-2411-273: Current video generation models primarily focus on overall visual fidelity and temporal smoothness without explicitly enforcing depth consistency between stereo views over time.
elements.sent-2411-274: Thus, existing methods lack mechanisms to dynamically adapt depth information during the diffusion process, which is necessary to accurately represent scene dynamics in stereo video generation.
elements.sent-2411-275: To address these challenges, we introduce a novel framework for zero-shot stereo video generation that leverages video diffusion priors to generate high-quality stereo videos without the need for paired training data.
elements.sent-2411-276: Instead of performing stereo conversion on the video level, we improve and refine the consistency within the latent features of a video diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-277: One significant advantage is that our method does not require precise disparity maps.
elements.sent-2411-278: We introduce the concept of dissolved depth maps, which retain only the low-frequency structural depth information.
elements.sent-2411-279: By emphasizing global structure over pixel-level depth variations, our approach reduces high-frequency noise that can lead to misalignment and artifacts in the generated right view.
elements.sent-2411-280: Furthermore, our approach seamlessly integrates advanced video depth estimation into the diffusion-based synthesis process, ensuring both depth consistency and temporal coherence.
elements.sent-2411-281: We introduce StereoCrafter-Zero, a novel approach for zero-shot stereo video generation based on video diffusion models without the need for paired training data.
elements.sent-2411-282: To enhance latent consistency, we employ a noisy restart strategy to obtain stereo-aware initial latents, followed by an iterative refinement process that systematically injects controlled noise into the diffusion process, progressively improving the harmony of the latent space.
elements.sent-2411-283: We introduce dissolved depth maps, a novel depth representation that retains only low-frequency structural depth, reducing high-frequency noise and improving stereo coherence.
elements.sent-2411-284: Our approach avoids reliance on accurate depth estimation, making the approach more robust.
elements.sent-2411-285: We conduct thorough evaluations, including statistical analysis and user studies, demonstrating the ability of our method to generate high-quality stereo videos.

Bottom: The proposed pipeline takes a conditioning image and text prompt as input, generating both left and right views that produce a strong stereoscopic effect.}
elements.sent-2411-286: Novel-view synthesis task aims to generate images from new camera perspectives based on one or more source images.

elements.sent-2411-288: However, these methods require scene-specific optimization, limiting their applicability to video data.

elements.sent-2411-290: These approaches suffer from visual artifacts in the inpainted regions, particularly in complex scenes with large disparities or occlusions.
elements.sent-2411-291: More importantly, these methods cannot enforce temporal consistency, which is an important requirement for handling video-based novel view synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-292: The Collaborative Video Diffusion (CVD) technique~\citep{kuang2024cvd utilized a cross-video synchronization module to directly generate multi-view videos for predefined camera trajectories.}
elements.sent-2411-293: Another line of work~\citep{yu20244real,bahmani20244dfy,zhang20244diffusion focuses on explicit 3D scene construction with temporal dynamics, enabling the generation of novel-view videos.}

elements.sent-2411-296: Stereo content generation has evolved significantly from traditional disparity estimation and view synthesis methods to modern deep learning approaches that enhance both depth accuracy and visual realism.
elements.sent-2411-297: Early approaches~\cite{xie2016deep3d, wang2019web utilized neural networks and generative networks to predict disparity maps and synthesize corresponding views, enabling the generation of stereo image pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-298: Recent advancements have extended these techniques to video by incorporating temporal coherence, thereby creating smooth and immersive stereo video sequences ~\cite{zhang2022temporal3d,shi2024immersepro, zhao2024stereocrafter .}
elements.sent-2411-299: Additionally, diffusion models have been successfully adapted for stereo image generation, enabling zero-shot synthesis~\cite{wang2024stereodiffusion .}
elements.sent-2411-300: However, the application of such zero-shot generative models for stereo video remains under exploration due to the additional challenges of ensuring both spatial depth fidelity and temporal consistency across frames.

elements.sent-2411-302: In contrast, our method generates new viewpoints without the need for warping at every step, which significantly reduces the computational costs.
elements.sent-2411-303: In this section, we describe the StereoCrafter-Zero framework for zero-shot stereo video generation, which leverages video diffusion priors to synthesize stereo-consistent videos.
elements.sent-2411-304: Let \(\mathbf{X = \{x_T, x_{T-1 , \dots, x_0\}\) denote the DDIM latent sequence from a video diffusion model with \(T\) diffusion steps (e.g., \(T=50\) ).}}
elements.sent-2411-305: Each latent \(x_t \in \mathbb{R ^{B \times C \times T \times H \times W}\) is a five-dimensional tensor representing a batch of video frames, where \(B\) is the batch size, \(C\) the number of channels, \(T\) the temporal dimension (number of frames), and \(H\) and \(W\) the spatial height and width, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-306: By decoding $x_0$, we obtain a video sequence $V\in \mathbb{R ^{B \times 3 \times T \times H' \times W'}$, where $H'$ and $W'$ are the corresponding height and width of the decoded videos.}
elements.sent-2411-307: To capture the scene geometry and enable accurate latent warping, we generate depth maps \(\mathbf{D \in \mathbb{R ^{B \times T \times H' \times W'}\), which provide per-pixel, temporally consistent depth information.}}
elements.sent-2411-308: Using these depth maps, the latent features are warped to achieve stereo consistency and account for parallax effects.
elements.sent-2411-309: Let \(\Delta\) denote the disparity map that is computed by~\cref{eq:disparity , which represents the horizontal shift between the left and right stereo views.}
elements.sent-2411-310: The warp operation \(\mathbf{W (x, \Delta)\) defined in ~\cref{eq:warp , is then applied to produce the warped latent representation $x^{\text{warp _{t =\mathbf{W (x_t, \Delta)$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-311: Details of our efficient implementation of the warping algorithm are provided in the supplementary material.
elements.sent-2411-312: This warping aligns the latent representations according to depth-induced disparities, which is essential for generating realistic stereo views.
elements.sent-2411-313: The warping process, however, introduces blank regions \(x^{blank \in \mathbb{R ^{B \times C \times T \times H \times W}\) in the warped latents due to occlusions or disocclusions, defined as: \begin{equation}x^{blank}_{t} = 1 ~~ \text{if}~ x^{\text{warp}}_{t} ~\text{ is undefined}, ~~~~~~0 ~~\text{otherwise}. \label{eq:blank_areas}\end{equation} With the aid of a disparity map, each latent feature can be decomposed into $x^{blank _{t}$ and $x^{\text{warp _{t}$ after warping.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-314: Maintaining consistency between these two latent parts is critical for creating meaningful and harmonized outputs for each individual view with correct depth cues.
elements.sent-2411-315: Since $\epsilon_t$ is the only source of randomness during the diffusion process \(refer to~\cref{eq:ddim}\), we, therefore, refine the latent features by iteratively injecting $\epsilon_t$ into the diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-316: The main contribution of our paper lies in improving consistency between $x^{blank _{t}$ and $x^{\text{warp _{t}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-317: We achieve this through two key steps: (1) Noisy Restart (\cref{sec:noisy_restart ), which initializes a reasonable $x^{blank}$, and (2) Iterative Refinement (\cref{sec:iter_refine ) that improves the consistency between $x^{blank}$ and $x^{warp}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-318: In addition, we introduce Dissolved Depth Maps (\cref{sec:dslv_depth ) which transform depth maps into lower-frequency representations to enhance the consistency of warped latents with the video diffusion prior.}
elements.figure-2411-021: \includegraphics[width=1.\linewidth]{misc/noisy_restart.drawio.pdf} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{-1.5em} \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{-0.5em} \caption{Illustration of the noisy start strategy. At selected steps, we replace the target view sampling with a warped source view. Occluded/disoccluded areas are then filled using the non-warped source view latent, with added noise injected into the latent space. Subsequent iterations update the latents with values from the preceding iteration, while preserving the non-occluded regions.} \label{fig:noisy_restart_fig}
elements.sent-2411-319: The noisy restart mechanism operates over $K$ diffusion steps for $L$ iterations.
elements.sent-2411-320: We denote the $K$ diffusion steps as $\{ x_t \}_{t=0 ^{K}$, where $x_t$ represents the latent state at step $t$.}
elements.sent-2411-321: While $K$ can be a set of discrete timesteps, we use $K=\{49,...,45\}$ in our implementation.
elements.sent-2411-322: The main purpouse of the noisy restart is to introduce random noise into the latent space to prevent structural repetition and ensure smooth transitions.
elements.sent-2411-323: In the first iteration, $K$ filling latents are generated for each diffusion step, while the subsequent iterations will refine the obtained $K$ latents.
elements.sent-2411-324: Referring to~\cref{eq:ddim , a random noise $\epsilon_t$ is introduced at each sampling step.}
elements.sent-2411-325: If each iteration uses different noise values, consistency across iterations can be disrupted.
elements.sent-2411-326: To address this issue, we initialize fixed noise tensors $\epsilon{' _t$ before starting the diffusion sampling sequence and use the same random seed for each iteration.This operation ensures that the random generator status remains the same across multiple iterations, and the noise injection is controlled solely by $\epsilon'_t$ and $\alpha_t$ in~\cref{eq:noisy_restart .}}
elements.sent-2411-327: Meanwhile, such a controlled approach promotes structural stability throughout the generation process, ensuring consistent low-frequency noise that preserves major features across frames.
elements.sent-2411-328: In our work, we use a total of $L = 7$ iterations, with the first iteration directly using the left-view latents as the filling latents.
elements.sent-2411-329: Noise is injected into the latent state through a weighted addition, balancing the contributions of the existing latent and the injected noise.
elements.sent-2411-330: The update equation for $x_{t-1}$ is given by: \begin{equation}x_{t-1} = x_{t} \cdot (1 - \alpha_t) + \sigma_t \epsilon{'}_t \alpha_t \label{eq:noisy_restart}\end{equation} where $\sigma_t $ is the noise magnitude for each timestep, scaling the impact of injected noise, and $\alpha_t$ is the balancing coefficient controlling the mix between the latent state and the noise.

elements.sent-2411-338: Since the noisy restart happens on the first several sampling steps, the stereo effects can be shaky and unstable across different timeframes.
elements.sent-2411-339: The iterative refinement is achieved by repeating the denoising operation at specific sampling steps.
elements.sent-2411-340: During these steps, the model performs multiple iterations to progressively enhance the latent representation.
elements.sent-2411-341: Since the diffusion model is not inherently aware of stereo effects, this mechanism enables the model to focus on refining details and correcting errors introduced by the video diffusion priors in the later steps.
elements.sent-2411-342: Starting from the second iteration $j > 1$, we update the filling area with the following equation: { \begin{equation} \hat{{x}}_0'^j = \begin{cases} \hat{{x}}_0'^{j} * \sqrt{\alpha_t} + \hat{{x}}_0'^{j-1} * (1 - \sqrt{\alpha_t}) \text{if } x_t^{blank}=1, \hat{{x}}_0'^{j-1} \text{otherwise}. \end{cases} \label{eq:blank_areas_itrefine}\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-343: Depth estimation models are typically designed to capture fine, detailed depth information.
elements.sent-2411-344: However, when warping the latent space of a diffusion model, these high-frequency details can compromise the latent space consistency.
elements.sent-2411-345: As shown in~\cref{sec:evaluation , high-precision depth maps often introduce artifacts, such as ghosting, during the warping process.}
elements.sent-2411-346: We suspect that these fine-grained warping interfere with temporal coherence, making them less compatible with the video diffusion priors.
elements.sent-2411-347: To address this issue, we propose a depth-dissolving technique that transforms the depth maps into a lower-frequency representation.
elements.sent-2411-348: Inspired by Dissolving Is Amplifying (DIA)~\cite{shi2024dissolving , we generate dissolved depth maps by leveraging the inherent properties of diffusion models to act as a low-pass filter on the latent space.}
elements.sent-2411-349: Using a diffusion-based depth estimation model, we first obtain a depth latent $x_T$ at the final diffusion step $T$.
elements.sent-2411-350: Instead of performing a full reverse diffusion process, we excute a single-step reverse diffusion on $x_T$.
elements.sent-2411-351: This approximation is designed to suppress high-frequency details, thereby reducing noise and artifacts in the latent representation.
elements.sent-2411-352: Formally, we denote the approximated initial state as $\hat{x _{t\rightarrow 0}$, which depends on the selected time step $t$.}
elements.sent-2411-353: The process is defined as follows: \begin{equation}\hat{x}_{t\rightarrow 0}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{\Bar{\alpha}_t}} \cdot x - \sqrt{\frac{1}{\Bar{\alpha}_t} - 1}\cdot \epsilon_\theta(x,t),\end{equation} where $\Bar{\alpha _t$ represents the cumulative product of the diffusion coefficients up to time $t$, $\epsilon_\theta$ is the predicted noise at time $t$.}
elements.sent-2411-354: By emphasizing global structure over pixel-level depth variations, our approach enables smoother disparity transitions.
elements.sent-2411-355: As a result, the warped latents maintain better temporal coherence and align more effectively with the video diffusion priors.
elements.sent-2411-356: Experimental results confirm our hypothesis that dissolved depth maps reduce artifacts such as ghosting and staircase effects.

elements.sent-2411-395: In this work, we introduced StereoCrafter-Zero, a novel zero-shot stereo video generation approach.
elements.sent-2411-396: StereoCrafter-Zero incorporates a noisy restart strategy for stereo-aware latent initialization and an iterative refinement process to enhance latent consistency by systematically injecting controlled noise.
elements.sent-2411-397: We also proposed dissolved depth maps that retains only low-frequency structural depth, reducing high-frequency noise and improving the coherence and stability of the stereoscopic effects.
elements.sent-2411-398: Our comprehensive evaluations, including statistical analysis and user studies, demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in generating high-quality stereo videos with enhanced depth consistency and temporal smoothness.
elements.sent-2411-399: Future research will focus on developing an adaptive method for determining the optimal dissolving level and exploring the incorporation of user guidance for personalized control over the generated stereo videos.
